summary: Gruber announces his inclusion in Joel Spolsky's compilation 'The Best Software Writing I,' with his essay 'The Location Field Is the New Command Line.' He notes the essay is freely available online and he received only nominal payment, but considers the inclusion an honor. He praises the book's typography, particularly its use of Sabon typeface. The post also celebrates a Father's Day gift of 'The Stanley Kubrick Archives' and humorously notes his friend Jim Coudal received the same book, and closes with a playful endorsement of a children's book.
key_points: Gruber's essay was selected by Spolsky, not self-nominated, and he considers it one of his pieces with lasting relevance
All essays in the book are freely available online, making the purchase purely about the curated collection and physical artifact
Gruber's standard for agreeing to participate was the book's visual design quality — he'd rather not be published than be published in a poorly-designed setting
The book is set in Sabon, Gruber's favorite typeface, leading to his formula: good writing + good typesetting = a pleasure to read
The Stanley Kubrick Archives is celebrated as a masterpiece of physical book design, complete with actual film strips from 2001
money_quotes: I'd rather not see my work published at all than see it published in a poorly-designed setting.
Good writing + good typesetting = a pleasure to read.
Sabon is the font family I'd choose to take with me to a desert island.
This book is why they make coffee tables.
It's one of those cases where it really is an honor just to be included.
He's writing acclaimed essays at an age when I did little more than sit around getting baked and playing NHL Hockey '93 on Sega Genesis.
Just in case Spolsky subsequently edits a second volume, I will say he's a very handsome and clever fellow.
key_insight: For Gruber, the physical design and typography of a book are not secondary concerns but essential to whether the writing is worth publishing at all.
